Row1.Pathway ID/Name: RAF–MEK–ERK (MAPK) core cascade in BRAF V600E‑mutant NSCLC
Row1.Rationale: BRAF V600E constitutively activates MEK/ERK; combined BRAF and MEK inhibition with dabrafenib+trametinib achieved an ORR of 64% and median PFS of 10.8 months in metastatic BRAF V600E‑mutant NSCLC, leading to FDA approval of the regimen, indicating strong pathway‑addicted sensitivity when MAPK output is suppressed at dual nodes.

Row2.Rationale: KRAS mutations activate MAPK signaling, but the phase II trial of trametinib versus docetaxel in KRAS‑mutant NSCLC showed no PFS or ORR advantage, reflecting limited clinical benefit from MEK blockade alone despite pathway inhibition, and supporting the need for rational combinations.

Row3.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant NSCLC, MEK1/2 inhibition disrupts PP2A carboxymethylation and destabilizes PP2A‑B56α complexes, promoting intrinsic resistance to RAS/MAPK inhibitors; pharmacologic PP2A stabilization with RPT04402 plus trametinib enhances apoptosis, induces durable tumor regressions, and delays acquired resistance in A549 xenografts, showing that intact PP2A feedback is required for sustained MAPK‑directed response.

Row4.Pathway ID/Name: PP2A–AKT/mTOR collateral survival pathway under MEK inhibition
Row4.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant lung cancer cells, genetic or functional PP2A inhibition prevents trametinib from fully suppressing ERK targets and induces a broad increase in AKT substrate phosphorylation; combining trametinib with mTOR (temsirolimus) or AKT (perifosine) inhibitors, or with PP2A‑activating SMAPs, restores cytotoxicity and overcomes MEK‑inhibitor resistance in vitro and in xenografts.

Row5.Pathway ID/Name: PPP6C–NF‑κB–Cyclin D1 axis under MAPK blockade
Row5.Rationale: PPP6C deletion in KRAS‑ and BRAF‑mutant models enhances NF‑κB signaling and sustains Cyclin D1 despite MEK/ERK inhibition, blunting trametinib efficacy; in xenografts, PPP6C‑knockout tumors are refractory to trametinib, whereas co‑inhibition of IKKβ/NF‑κB (TPCA‑1) with trametinib restores tumor control, implicating this phosphatase–NF‑κB axis as a determinant of MEK‑inhibitor sensitivity.

Row6.Pathway ID/Name: METex14–KRAS–MEK/ERK signaling in MET TKI resistance
Row6.Rationale: In MET exon14‑mutant lung cancers, concurrent or acquired KRAS activation maintains RAS/ERK signaling and mediates primary and secondary resistance to MET TKIs; in patient‑derived LUAD12C and engineered METex14/KRAS models, crizotinib or cabozantinib plus trametinib shows synergistic growth inhibition, and in xenografts, trametinib combined with crizotinib or afatinib markedly enhances tumor control versus monotherapy.

Row7.Rationale: In KRAS‑mutant METex14 NSCLC models, trametinib suppresses MYC but induces transcriptional upregulation and phosphorylation of EGFR, ERBB2, ERBB3 and AKT, partly via increased ligands (TGF‑α, EGF, BTC, NRG1), creating an autocrine ERBB–PI3K survival loop; combining trametinib with EGFR/ERBB2 inhibition (afatinib) plus MET TKI deepens signaling suppression and improves xenograft responses.

Row8.Pathway ID/Name: KRAS G12V–driven MEK/ERK activation in EGFR‑mutant leptomeningeal NSCLC
Row8.Rationale: In an EGFR‑mutant leptomeningeal carcinomatosis model, acquired KRAS G12V maintains downstream MEK/ERK signaling and confers resistance to osimertinib despite persistent EGFR exon19 deletion; KRAS knockdown restores sensitivity, and co‑treatment with trametinib and osimertinib overcomes resistance in vitro and effectively controls leptomeningeal disease in vivo.

Row9.Pathway ID/Name: HDAC3–NKX2‑1–FGFR1 transcriptional program in KRAS/LKB1‑mutant NSCLC
Row9.Rationale: In Kras/Lkb1‑mutant lung cancer GEMMs, HDAC3 cooperates with lineage TF NKX2‑1 to drive a shared transcriptional program, including FGFR1, that becomes hyperactivated as tumors acquire resistance to trametinib; the HDAC1/3 inhibitor entinostat reverses this cassette, and entinostat plus trametinib provides therapeutic benefit in vivo, implicating an epigenetically reinforced FGFR1 axis in MEK‑inhibitor resistance.

Row10.Rationale: MET exon14 skipping stabilizes MET and activates downstream RAS/ERK; gene‑set enrichment of METex14 lung tumors shows KRAS pathway activation, and in METex14 models with KRAS alterations, dual MET and MEK inhibition (crizotinib or cabozantinib plus trametinib) yields synergistic viability reduction and significant xenograft tumor growth inhibition compared with either agent alone.

Row11.Pathway ID/Name: BRAF–MEK–ERK pathway in oncogene‑driven NSCLC (pan‑driver context)
Row11.Rationale: Across oncogene‑driven NSCLC, MAPK activation is a common downstream node; dabrafenib+trametinib is now standard for BRAF V600E disease, while MEK‑based combinations (e.g., with KRAS G12C or MET inhibitors) are being explored to overcome resistance in other driver subsets, reflecting the centrality but context‑dependent druggability of this pathway.

Row12.Pathway ID/Name: PI3K–AKT–mTOR pathway as parallel survival route during MAPK inhibition
Row12.Rationale: Reviews of NSCLC resistance mechanisms highlight PI3K/AKT/mTOR as a frequent bypass pathway when upstream RTKs or MAPK are inhibited; activation via PTEN loss, KRAS co‑mutation or RTK feedback can blunt MEK‑directed therapies, supporting combined MAPK and PI3K/mTOR targeting or vertical RTK/MAPK blockade to enhance trametinib‑based regimens.
